sound_type: amplified krek-krek wall of sound -- collective breeding chorus, hundreds or thousands calling simultaneously from marsh edges on spring nights, NOT an individual call but a wall of sound, E=4, marsh habitat
ecosystem: Marsh. Freshwater marshes, ponds, wetlands, wet meadows. Also gardens, forests, grasslands. Breeds in standing water. Remarkably adaptable. Consecutive marsh species (85-86).
season: Late winter through spring (breeding chorus). Active year-round in mild climates. Nocturnal during breeding season.

species_pair.pair_type: Consecutive marsh species (85-86). Muskrat (quiet plop, E=3, Mammalia) and Pacific Treefrog (loud chorus, E=4, Amphibia). Two classes sharing the same marsh.

coordinates.description: Reference for marsh habitat in the Puget Sound lowlands, Washington. Pacific Treefrog chorus audible at wetlands throughout the region.

taxonomy_note.description: AMPHIBIA RETURNS. First amphibian in the engine in its own right. After 25 consecutive mammalian entries (61-85) and 16 bird-to-bird substitutions replacing CSV amphibians (45-60). NEW CLASS re-entering. NEW FAMILY Hylidae (37th). NEW ORDER Anura. NEW GENUS Pseudacris. Breaks Cricetidae streak (83-84-85).
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Amphibia does not use infraclass in the same way. Lissamphibia is the subclass.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Pseudacris from Greek pseudes ('false') + akris ('locust'), referring to the insect-like call. regilla from Latin 'regal' or 'little queen.' Formerly Hyla regilla. Reclassified based on molecular phylogenetics.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Pseudacris -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Chorus frogs. First anuran genus.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anura -- first Anura (frogs) in the engine. NEW ORDER.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Amphibia -- RETURNS. Third class in the engine (after Aves and Mammalia). First amphibian entry.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Hylidae's FIRST entry at degree 86. NEW FAMILY (37th).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Individual call: approximately 1,000-4,000 Hz. Peak at approximately 2,000-2,500 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Chorus peak: approximately 2,000-3,000 Hz. The massed chorus creates a broad peak in this band.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. BREEDING CHORUS. Individual call: 'krek-ek, krek-ek' -- two-note, rapidly repeated.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual call: approximately 0.1-0.2 seconds per 'krek-ek.' Repeated continuously. Chorus: hours-long, from dusk through night.
song_metrics.note_structure: Two-note: 'krek-ek.' First note slightly higher, second note slightly lower. Rapidly repeated. Males call from partially submerged positions at marsh edges.
song_metrics.note_types: Krek-ek (primary advertisement call), trilling (extended call variant), encounter call (shorter, used in male-male competition).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual: continuous repetition of krek-ek. Chorus: hundreds of individuals calling asynchronously, creating a continuous wall of sound with no gaps.
song_metrics.timbre: Individual: sharp, bright, penetrating. Insect-like (hence Pseudacris = 'false locust'). Chorus: dense, layered, continuous. A wall of sound that fills the marsh.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Individual calls are loud for body size (approximately 80 dB at 1 m). The chorus is LOUD -- audible from hundreds of meters. Dominates the marsh soundscape on spring nights. The most iconic sound of PNW wetlands.
song_metrics.tempo: Individual: approximately 2-3 calls per second. Chorus: continuous. The asynchronous calling creates a pulsing, continuous texture.
song_metrics.context: BREEDING CHORUS: males call to attract females. Night chorus from marsh edges. Late winter through spring. Peaks on warm, humid nights. The collective chorus is the primary acoustic phenomenon -- individual calls blend into a wall of sound.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal variation in call structure across range. Chorus density varies by population size and habitat quality.
song_metrics.key_property: AMPLIFIED KREK-KREK WALL OF SOUND -- the 87th acoustic element. Collective breeding chorus. NOT an individual call but a wall of sound. Hundreds calling simultaneously. E=4. The most iconic sound of PNW wetlands. Used in films to signify 'nighttime outdoors.' AMPHIBIA RETURNS.

physical_description.overall: Tiny tree frog. 3-5 cm (1-2 inches). 2-5 g. One of the smallest vertebrates in the engine. Highly variable coloration -- green, brown, tan, or gray. Can change color slowly (hours to days). The defining diagnostic is the dark eye stripe.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 86: (1) DARK EYE STRIPE -- a dark stripe from nostril through eye to shoulder. The key field mark. Present in all color morphs. (2) TOE PADS -- expanded adhesive toe pads for climbing. Typical of tree frogs. (3) SMALL SIZE -- 3-5 cm. Tiny. (4) VARIABLE COLOR -- green, brown, tan, gray. Can change. (5) VOCAL SAC -- males have a large, dark vocal sac that inflates during calling.
physical_description.skin_detail: BODY: Smooth, moist skin. Highly variable in color -- green, brown, tan, reddish, or gray. Can change color over hours to days in response to temperature and humidity. BELLY: Cream to white. STRIPE: Dark stripe from nostril through eye to shoulder -- the defining field mark.
physical_description.field_id: Tiny frog (3-5 cm) with a dark eye stripe from nostril through eye to shoulder. Variable coloration (green, brown, tan). Expanded toe pads. Most often detected by the CHORUS -- the krek-ek breeding call on spring nights. The sound is far more detectable than the animal. Tiny frog, huge voice.

nodes.title: Amphibia Returns -- Class Barrier Breaks
nodes.description: First amphibian in the engine in its own right. After 25 consecutive mammalian entries (61-85) and 16 bird substitutions replacing amphibians (45-60). Amphibia re-enters. Third class in the engine.

nodes.title: Krek-Krek Night Chorus -- 87th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Collective breeding chorus. Hundreds calling simultaneously. Wall of sound. E=4. The most iconic sound of PNW wetlands. Used in films to signify 'nighttime outdoors.'

nodes.title: Hylidae -- 37th Family -- New Family, New Class
nodes.description: NEW FAMILY. Tree frog family. 37th family in engine. Breaks Cricetidae streak (83-85). Crosses class boundary from Mammalia to Amphibia.

nodes.title: Consecutive Marsh Species (85-86)
nodes.description: Muskrat (85) and Pacific Treefrog (86). Consecutive marsh species. Mammal and amphibian sharing the same marsh. Quiet plop (E=3) then loud chorus (E=4).

nodes.title: The Most Iconic PNW Wetland Sound
nodes.description: The Pacific Treefrog chorus is the sound used in Hollywood films, TV, and media to signify 'nighttime outdoors.' Even in scenes set far from the PNW, the krek-ek call is the go-to 'frog sound.'

nodes.title: The Postal Service Parallel
nodes.description: Both walls of sound. Both E=4. Both collective -- electronic layers vs frog chorus. Both create something larger than any individual element.

nodes.title: Twenty-Sixth Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-six consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. The CSV had Pacific Chorus Frog at degree 45 (substituted). This is degree 86 -- a DIFFERENT placement. Original.

cross_references.relationship: MARSH SHARED. Consecutive marsh species (85-86). Muskrat (quiet plop, E=3, Mammalia) and treefrog (loud chorus, E=4, Amphibia). Two classes sharing the same marsh. The plop and the chorus on the same spring night.
MARSH SHARED. Both in marsh/pond habitat. Mallard (Aves) and treefrog (Amphibia). Three classes sharing the marsh by degree 86: Aves, Mammalia, Amphibia.
POND/MARSH. Both in marsh-adjacent aquatic habitat. Wood Duck (pond) and treefrog (marsh edge). Both breed in spring near standing water.
POND/MARSH BUILDER. Beaver creates ponds that become treefrog breeding habitat. The dam builder creates the chorus stage. Ecological engineering enables the night chorus.
PREY CONTRAST. Both common, both heavily preyed upon. Different trophic levels and habitats. Hare in forest understory, treefrog at marsh edge.
